name: scenario_1
intro: The EM Physician has told you that you will be getting a history from a 22-year-old male with autism spectrum disorder presenting with abdominal pain. The ED is busy, loud, and bright. You discuss with the nurse before entering the patient room and he tells you he attempted IV placement, which caused the patient to become distressed and yell “Stop!” He lets you know that the patient remains visibly agitated and sensitive to the surrounding noise and activity.
goal: Your goal is to reduce the patient’s agitation using appropriate de-escalation strategies before proceeding with further care.

actions.desc: Ask caregiver for guidance or involve them in calming
actions.scene_change: Patient stops yelling, looks toward caregiver, posture softens

actions.desc: Dim lights, reduce noise, limit staff, pause IV
actions.scene_change: Lights dim, noise decreases, fewer staff present, patient less overstimulated
Patient quieter, less defensive, brief eye contact
Patient engages slightly, less tension in body

actions.desc: Acknowledge that IV will be done later
actions.scene_change: Staff step back, patient relaxes, less defensive movement

actions.desc: Offer water/food; comforting words that do not translate to an available action
actions.scene_change: No meaningful change; patient remains distressed
Patient yells loudly, pulls away, rapid escalation
Caregiver appears frustrated, patient agitation increases

actions.desc: E.g. “We need to do this now”
actions.scene_change: Increased yelling, resistance, possible attempt to leave
Patient covers ears, increased distress and movement
END SIMULATION - Ensure feedback includes a discussion of how there is a better approach and/or this level of deescalation would not be the most ethical choice in the scenario
